To cover the costs of reconstruction and support for victims, Greece will replace the previous bed tax with a new "climate resilience levy" starting in 2024. This fee will vary depending on the travel season. From March to October, the new tourist tax will increase accommodation costs. The fees for hotels will increase as follows: 1.50 euros per night for one or two-star hotels (previously 0.50 euros), 3 euros per night for three-star hotels (previously 1.50 euros), 7 euros per night for four-star hotels (previously 3 euros), and 10 euros per night for five-star hotels (previously 4 euros). The Greek government expects this new regulation to generate up to 300 million euros in additional revenue in 2024. Unlike the previous accommodation tax, the new tax will also apply to short-term rentals booked through online platforms. The "Ditch the Witch" slogan, used during protests against the Gillard government's carbon tax, became one of the most contentious moments in modern Australian political history. Critics condemned the language as overtly sexist, arguing it would never have been directed at a male Prime Minister and that it reflected a pattern of gendered hostility that Julia Gillard faced throughout her time in office. The slogan appeared on protest signs alongside senior opposition figures, fuelling accusations that the language had been tacitly endorsed by mainstream political leadership. Those who believe the campaign was sexist argue that the word "witch" is a gendered slur with centuries of history targeting women, that the broader treatment of Gillard in public discourse was shaped by her gender in ways that male leaders never experience, and that the failure of opposition leaders to explicitly condemn the language amounted to complicity. Defenders of the campaign argue that political protest has always used strong language against sitting leaders regardless of gender, that the anger was directed at a specific policy broken promise rather than at Gillard's gender, and that labelling legitimate political opposition as sexism is a tactic used to silence critics rather than address their arguments. #Australia #DitchTheWitch #JuliaGillard #Sexism #FrontlineAustralia #AustralianPolitics #GenderPolitics #CarbonTax #PoliticalProtest #PolicyDebate

cont_clean: Singapore's carbon tax just jumped from SGD 25 to SGD 45 per tonne. By 2030, it is set to reach SGD 50 to 80. For a typical 50,000 sqm Grade A office, that is roughly SGD 130,000 to SGD 150,000 of new annual cost, passed through your electricity bill. By 2030, two to three times that. The compliance frameworks already overlap. The Carbon Pricing Act 2018, BCA Green Mark 2021, and ISO 50001 all ask for the same evidence: continuous, sub-system metering with a clean audit trail. One setup serves all three. A single main meter no longer cuts it. It cannot show which loads are driving emissions, or prove the savings from any retrofit you fund. Heading into a Green Mark re-certification, a first SGX climate disclosure, or a tenant ESG questionnaire? The metering layer is the gating step. Start the audit nine months before the filing, not in the final quarter. SGX-listed companies already file Scope 1 and 2 from FY 2025. Large non-listed companies follow from FY 2030. We wrote up the five EMS capabilities the 2026 rules now ask for, plus the three gaps we see most often on Singapore commercial buildings. topic: 美国是否应扩大液化天然气 LNG 出口？
cont_clean: Prior to 2016, only small volumes of liquefied natural gas (LNG) were shipped from the United States. The first US LNG cargo crossed the Atlantic in 1959, but the trade proved uneconomical. Later, a small-capacity liquefaction terminal (about 0.2 bcf/d [5.6 x 106 m3/d]) was built in Kenai, Alaska, which shipped about 1300 cargoes to Japan between 1969 and 2011. Sabine Pass LNG Terminal in Louisiana, operated by #cheniereenergy began operations as an import terminal in 2008. The rapid increase of oil and natural gas production from shale resources in the mid-2000s, however, curbed US LNG import demand and led terminal operators to convert some LNG import infrastructure into export infrastructure. On February 24, 2016, the first export cargo of the shale era departed aboard the Asia Vision for Brazil, carrying 3.3 bcf (93.4 x 106 m3) of LNG. Since then, Sabine Pass has shipped over 3300 cargoes worldwide (39% of all US export cargoes through November 2025), according to the US Department of Energy's @energy LNG Exports and Re-Exports Details. The WHRI team spoke with Dr. Sarah Munro and Kaiya Jacob who are apart of the Reproductive Choices Lab. The lab brings together the health and research community to generate evidence supporting individuals to make informed reproductive choices. One of the lab's initiatives is the Ask US project where they are seeking to understand youth attitudes toward contraception and how policy can adapt to improve contraception access specifically for youth. We're proud of the work that WHRI researchers have accomplished thus far in the field of contraception, and look forward to even greater advancements. To learn more about this pivotal work by the Reproductive Choices Lab and how research is advancing contraception access, education and breaking stigma — click the link in bio. Photo credit: Jesseca Buzoin jayymadethis #WomensResearch #WorldContraceptionDay #WomensHealth #ContraceptionAccess #LARCs #IUDs

cont_clean: New Research Brief⁠ ⁠ Access to contraception doesn't only depend on policy — it also depends on whether people can actually find it at their local pharmacy.⁠ ⁠ A new brief from Resound Research for Reproductive Health examines availability, accessibility, and affordability of emergency contraception and over-the-counter hormonal contraception (Opill®) in Texas pharmacies.⁠ ⁠ By visiting 192 pharmacies throughout the state, researchers assessed whether these methods were available for purchase, accessible on the shelf, and affordable for Texans who may face barriers to clinic-based care or not have health insurance.⁠ ⁠ Over-the-counter contraception has the potential to expand access — but only if people can actually find it when they need it.⁠ ⁠ Our findings highlight how pharmacy availability and product accessibility can shape real-world access to contraception.⁠ ⁠ 🧾 Read the brief at the link in bio⁠ .⁠ .⁠ .⁠ #ReproductiveHealth #ContraceptionAccess #PublicHealthResearch⁠ #ReproHealth #HealthPolicy⁠

cont_clean: The world may feel heavy right now. And in women's health, safety and equality, there is no shortage of challenges. The gaps remain serious, systemic, and unacceptable. So it's all the more important to recognise when progress happens. For the first time in more than 30 years, new contraceptive options were added to the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme (PBS) in 2025, improving affordability for many women. Medications such as: • Yaz and Yasmin reduced from around $316 per year to $126.40, or $30.80 with a concession card. • The NuvaRing reduced from over $270 per year to $31.60 per script. • Long-acting contraceptives, including IUDs, became more accessible, with out-of-pocket costs reduced by up to $400. • From January 2026, PBS scripts are capped at a maximum of $25. In the past year, more than 303,000 women have accessed these lower-cost options. This progress matters. Cost is a well-documented barrier to accessing contraception, and reducing this barrier supports greater autonomy and choice. At the same time, gaps in women's healthcare remain, and continued advocacy is essential. Recognising progress does not mean the work is done. But it does show that sustained advocacy and policy change can lead to meaningful improvements in access and equity. #WomensHealth #WomensHealthEast #ReproductiveHealth #ContraceptionAccess #HealthEquity #PBS #AustralianWomen
